badges.title: Badges
badges.hint: Keep running and racing to unlock more — walks count too.

history.title: History
history.pendingSync: Saved on this device — will sync to the cloud when possible.

stats.title: Stats
stats.empty: Record some runs to see your stats!

predictions.title: Race predictions
predictions.subtitle: Projected from your strongest run in the selected window
predictions.needRun: Log a run of 3 km or more to estimate your race times.
predictions.bestEffortExplainer: <0>Best-effort</0> projects your strongest run {{run}} to each distance with Riegel's formula.
predictions.hrExplainer: <0>HR-modelled</0> fits your pace against heart rate across {{n}} runs and extrapolates to threshold effort (~{{thr}} bpm) — so easy runs handled well count too, not just your fastest day.
predictions.hrLocked: Add your max HR in Settings and log more runs across easy + hard efforts to unlock the HR-based estimate.
predictions.gradeNoteClimb: Runs are grade-adjusted for elevation gain. Times are for a flat course; the race-day row includes its {{m}} m climb.
predictions.gradeNoteFlat: Runs are grade-adjusted for elevation gain. Times are for a flat course, except the race-day row once you set its climb in the Plan settings.

info.title: How predictions work
info.label: How does this work?
info.intro: Predictions project your finish time at each distance from the runs you've actually logged. Two independent methods are shown so you can sanity-check one against the other.
info.riegelP1: Both estimates extrapolate a known time to a new distance with Peter Riegel's endurance model:
info.riegelP2: The <0>1.06</0> exponent is the standard fatigue factor: going further costs slightly more than linear time, because you can't hold a 5 km pace for 20 km.
info.bestBody: Picks your single <0>strongest run</0> (≥ 3 km with a time) and projects it to every distance. It isn't simply your fastest pace — a quick 1 km blip shouldn't outrank a strong 12 km — so each run is first normalised to its Riegel-equivalent 10 km time, and the best one wins.
info.hrP1: Fits a line through <0>pace vs. heart rate</0> across all your runs that recorded HR, then reads off the pace you could hold at threshold effort (~88–90% of max HR, roughly a one-hour race) and projects that with Riegel.
info.hrP2: This rewards efficiency: a fast pace held at a low HR pulls the estimate quicker, so your easy runs count too — not just your single best day. It only appears once the data supports it (≥ 8 HR runs, a real spread of efforts, and a sensible fit).
info.elevBody: Hills make a run slower than its flat twin, so each metre climbed is credited as ~8 extra flat metres before the maths runs. This stops hilly runs from looking unfit. Listed times are for a flat course; the <0>race-day row</0> adds back your course's climb once you set it in the Plan.
info.footer: These are projections from past efforts, not promises — treat them as a fitness gauge that sharpens as you log more runs.

zones.title: Heart Rate Zones

descs.2: Easy runs, fat burning, building endurance base
descs.3: Moderate effort — marathon and long-run pace
descs.4: Comfortably hard — lactate threshold, tempo runs
descs.5: Maximum effort — short intervals, race pace

zones.karvonenBody: Zone HR = ((MaxHR - RestHR) x intensity%) + RestHR. HRR = {{max}} - {{rest}} = {{hrr}} bpm. Accounts for your resting HR and fitness, so it's more accurate than plain % of Max HR.

planInfo.title: How your plan is built
planInfo.label: How is this built?
planInfo.intro: Your plan is generated from three inputs — your <0>race date</0>, your <1>goal time / distance</1>, and the <2>days you can train</2>. Everything below is derived from those; nothing is hand-picked.
planInfo.s1P1: Your goal time ÷ distance gives the pace you need on race day. If the course climbs, each metre of ascent is counted as ~8 extra flat metres, so a hilly goal demands the fitness of a faster flat runner. That <0>flat-equivalent target pace</0> anchors every session.
planInfo.s1P2: From the target pace we derive the training paces:
planInfo.s1Easy: <0>Easy</0> ≈ target × 1.25 — conversational, builds the aerobic engine
planInfo.s1Intervals: <0>Intervals</0> ≈ target pace — race effort in short reps
planInfo.s2P1: The calendar between next Monday and your race is split into 4–24 weeks, grouped into four phases that shift the emphasis over time:
planInfo.s2Base: <0>Base</0> (first ~4 weeks) — easy volume only, laying foundation
planInfo.s2Build: <0>Build</0> (middle block) — tempo and interval sessions alternate week to week
planInfo.s2Peak: <0>Peak</0> (final weeks before taper) — highest volume and sharpest workouts
planInfo.s2Taper: <0>Taper</0> (last 3 weeks) — volume drops to ~85 / 65 / 45% so you arrive fresh
planInfo.s3Body: Your longest available day becomes the <0>long run</0>; its distance grows week to week and is capped by the minutes you said you have (time ÷ easy pace). Your other days become the <1>quality</1> sessions — easy in base/taper, tempo or intervals in build. Every distance is clamped so a session never exceeds the time you committed to.
planInfo.footer: Changing the goal, date, elevation, or training days and regenerating rebuilds the whole schedule from scratch.
